speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four. Today's date is March 27, 1996. We are conducting an interview with the survivor Mr. Marvin Zborowski. Mr. Zborowski, your brother Eli suggested because you were lame that he would take Mother and Sister to the village into hiding, and he will come for you separately. What happens then?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did not mean exactly go to the village-- would be too far. No, what he meant to say is the following. We started to walk-- let me just retract a little bit. We started to walk. It was no way that we could walk all four of us. It was too obvious.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he suggested that Mother and Sister should go with him, let's just say for 500 yards of-- or half a mile, half a kilometer, what distance, let's just say, he pinpointed. He said, '15 minutes. Let me walk with them 15 minutes. I will come back 15 minutes, and then I walk with you. And then we go steps by step, all right?' So I was left alone. He walks away. I'm counting 15 minutes, another 15 minutes. I don't see nobody.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK. They didn't want to leave me, but I'm alone. Realizing that that's it. Maybe after an hour, I hear a whistle. Subconsciously-- it was dark already. Between the woods, it definitely was dark. And subconsciously, I-- I replied. And this was the lucky sign that he get closer where he heard the whistle. He said, 'I couldn't find you.' So he found me. He said, 'I couldn't--' Then he started to carry me. I was quite heavy. He started to schlep me. It was not an easy thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're walking maybe for a half an hour. I said, 'Eli, where's Mother, and where's our Sister?' He said, 'I'll tell you the truth. I don't know.' So he said, 'Look, I can tell you one thing. If I find now my mother and sister, we're not going to separate. We're going to walk as long as it may take, but we're not going to go search for each other, because we're losing the time.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, that's what had happened. When we found Mother, which was quite difficult in the darkness, in the woods, to find, in the forest, we finally starting to walk. It was very difficult to walk, because it was up the hill, down the hill.

speaker: interviewee
text: I kept on asking Eli, 'How far is it?' He says, 'You see over there, the light? That's the light. That's the house.' We got over there. He said, 'No, you see, that's the house, another--'

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, it took us practically a whole night until we made it to that village. When we came in there to the house, the man's name was [? Maria ?] and Jozef Kolacz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the village was Bobolice, B-O-B-O-L-I-C-E. Now, that-- in that village, in that house was-- were already some relatives-- two aunts, and an uncle-- two uncles, two aunts, and a cousin-- and cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their first names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their first names was Celia and husband [? Sam ?] Jonisch, my uncle, brother of my mother, Joseph Dauman, and her sister [? Rachel ?] Dauman, and cousin, [? Gosche ?] and David Jonisch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now describe the place where all of you were hiding now.

speaker: interviewee
text: The place where we got there, it was practically a chicken coop. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it together with the chickens?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The chickens were already-- the Jews were the chickens.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no chickens anymore. It was a small little window, small opening. The door that we walked in is from the main room. There was only one room that the man had. On one side was the stable, on one side was this chicken coop. And in that part, we were there. It was quite narrow. We had-- when we lay down to sleep, 10 of us, 10 Jews, this was really just barely that one was next to the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the chicken coop have anything on the floor? Were you lying on something special, or was it just the earth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's straw, I would say, straw. But being there under straw in some kind of type of cover of [NON-ENGLISH], how they would say it. And it was unbearable, especially the first days that we were there. The walls were infested with bedbugs, with all kind of insects, which was, in the beginning, untolerable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the hygienic conditions were even worse than on the one that we were at the 18 months that we were at the Placzek at the Zarki hiding. Over there was not the question of washing. The question was over there, there was no water. There was not the question of washing. It was the question, how do we get water to boil potatoes? Was this place a farmhouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a farmhouse and seemingly was on a hilly side where the water has to be brought in quite a few kilometers into-- to the house. So everything, really, had to be carried from far distance, actually, in barrels, or-- all means, how to get the water. So the water was the precious things. So just to give you, for an example, when water was-- when-- when the man of the house, so to say-- when he boiled his meal, or his potatoes, and the potatoes were ready, the water was not spilled. We just add up a little bit more water, and we boil the next shift of potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do about washing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very barely, I could remember of any washing. I would say that most of my body was like having leprosy. All the joints were really decaying, so to say. Pus underneath, pus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any remedy against the lice, or the insects, or the vermin?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not. Tried to eliminate them, but this was practically impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the day. Did you spend it all the time in the chicken coop, or were you allowed to go out into the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was a little bit tolerant. In other words, in the village, you could have moved around-- not on the outside, of course-- in the-- in the parameters of the-- of the house, which it was one room. We could walk into his room. We could go into the stable, where the horse was there, where-- where their rabbits were there. He was having rabbits, so I played with rabbits. I mean, I was a baby. I was looking at the rabbits. Because I was a baby, I was a boy. Then I was allowed to get into the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Into the barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into the barn. So I walked in there. So I was playing there. I mean, it was a little bit more airy. It was a little bit more fresh air.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't you afraid, or wasn't he afraid of detection by some of the neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normally, they did not visit each other. It was not that type, the peasants, that they were getting together. And we do recall that we had one really, a very serious incident. The serious incident was that he went to church on Sunday. And when he went to church, nobody was in the house. He went with his family. So we, in turn, watched the house. We have a signal not to open the door. The door was not locked by any means of hermetically locked, but just a little latch over it. So we had a system to knock three knocks at the door. And as I stated before, we could not eat together, or boil, or prepare the food together. So we were having in shifts-- four, four, and two. In other words, four-- the four Zborowski family, four, the other two sisters and their husbands and brothers, and the two cousins, the last ones.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they-- we were finished already, the-- luckily, everybody was inside in the chicken coop finished already or resting, I don't know what. I myself with my sister were in that big room. And I was looking out outside if somebody is coming in. And I was looking up front, not realizing that somebody may walk in through the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody came in through the back, knocks at the door three times, and my poor sister opens up the door. A man comes in. He was flabbergasted seeing us. We were speechless seeing him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, 'Is Mr. Kolacz home?' We says, 'No, he went to church.' He said goodbye.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't have to tell you that this was really Tisha B'Av, the day really of crying and lamenting. What would did we do? Of course, we did not blame Sister for it, but we really were scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he came home, we had to confess to the man. We cannot just simply say nothing happened. We told him. He said, 'Look, I'm very upset about it, but I have to think something about it. I have to do some really thinking.' He liked very much my brother, and he spoke to my brother. And they came back after discussion that the best thing will be to, at night, at the late hour, meaning not, say, before midnight, that we should go out to that group of houses in the village, of the group of village that we are, and scare off a little bit the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the 'we?'

speaker: interviewee
text: Meaning 'we,' he meant to say the following. Nobody knew Eli, my brother. He looked like a Gentile in the beginning. He had blonde hair, and so on. I had another uncle had also very light complexion. And he said, and he organized, that my brother, two of my uncles, and myself is four. He himself is five. One man was sick-- he couldn't go, so we were five. And he said, 'We have to go five of us and showing that we are some type of partisans, or pretending that we are partisans, and to scare off the people in that group of houses.' Scaring off, not by, God forbid, of doing something bad to them, just scaring them off. There should be no denunciation of any thinking and any collaborations with the Germans. It was a crazy idea, we really thought. But it turned out to be it was a very wise idea. The man, which I have to say was illiterate, but was highly intelligent, if he could have thought of-- of hiding, and helping, and feed 10 Jews. How to bring in food to one of the various remote places where everything, that-- that a cup of water was a problem. And to-- and to come up with that idea of saving Jews, and then come and go out and scaring his, so to say, his people-- it was a great idea. So we decided. And he said, 'I want you to go into each house, not to ask for anything.' Usually, the partisans in the past used to come into houses asking for food, for-- for chickens, for all kinds of means of food to be able to feed the partisans. So we did not ask for anything. We did not want anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were really the good partisans, so to say. We did not want anything. So what did we want to do? So when Eli walked in, into a house, he just asked, 'What is the name of this household, and how many people live in that household? And in case of an invasion of the troops, how many people would they able to station here?' That's about all, and wishing them all the best. How-- did all of you walk in with Eli, or just Eli alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. If we would have walked in with my other two uncles, they would definitely see that they are Jews. There's no two ways about it, even including myself, which I was an invalid. I was barely walking. I mean, I was already a few months later-- I was barely walking properly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I was-- so he said no. He said, 'I will guide you. I'm walking with you. Only Eli will go in.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was not Eli. His name was [? Ignacz. ?] And Uncle Joe, he will go in. And you will write, you will speak.'

speaker: interviewer
text: But the peasant himself did not walk in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no way. No way. But the only thing-- he looked into the windows.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when he walked in, so my brother's starting to say, 'What is the name of the household, and how many people?' And again and again, the same thing. And then he wrote down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we moved into the next house. We moved into the next house and did the same thing. Then all of a sudden, the peasant that he was hiding us said, 'Look, there are strange people and there's some girls over there. You know, some girls are playing around there with the men, I mean. What are those girls-- those girls don't belong in that house.'

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, 'Call your brother out.' So I'm not going to say, 'Brother, come out.' So I said it in a-- in a military way. He told me how to say [NON-ENGLISH], meaning, just 'Move out.'

speaker: interviewee
text: He moved out. And I said to my brother, I said, 'You know, Mr. Kolacz is saying that those girls don't belong in this house. But they identify them, try to do that.'

speaker: interviewee
text: He walked over.  He said, 'I want you to identify yourself. Are you strangers in this town, are you village?' 'No, I'm from the same houses.'

speaker: interviewee
text: The next Sunday-- so we scared them off. We didn't take anything. As a matter of fact, at one of the last places that we went, the people were already appreciating that we're giving them so much courage that the war is towards the end-- and it was, so to say, towards the end-- that they gave us, they prepared already for Christmas the special cake, which is the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stollen.

speaker: interviewee
text: --special cake made for Christmas. He said, 'You know what? We will share it with you. Take at least part of it, of the holiday cake that we baked. You are our-- our brothers, and let's hope and pray that the war will be over soon.' And the next Sunday, when the peasant again, now, Mr. Kolacz, went to church, the priest even said, 'Look, this week we had visitors. The real partisans were here. They did not plunder, they did not ask for anything, but they only said that we should be patient. We should not denounce each other. We should be happy, and that the war will be coming to an end.' This was the real partisan that was us.

speaker: interviewer
text: That is a fantastic story indeed. Now, how long did you stay with Mr. Kolacz, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there for almost a half a year. This was already before Christmas. We were liberated in January 17, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please describe that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were liberated by the Russians. And the night of the liberation were a very emotional one and a very exciting one. The area-- like I stated, it was closed already to the German border. And it was very fierce fightings. And there was some trenches made around, not exactly in the village, but on the outside-- double trenches, so to say. In the trenches were full of Germans. In the night, we did not know. We did not have any radios or any means that the-- that the front, so to say, that the army is five kilometers or 10 kilometers away. But we-- the area was very much indeed with full of partisans. And we did not know which one is the real one, which one is the other ones. There was-- was an Armia Krajowa, and there was Armia Ludowa, which it means was-- one was a AK army--

speaker: interviewer
text: AK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --which was-- which was sympathetic to the Jews, and one was not sympathetic to the   Jews. So the Armia Krajowa definitely was not, and Armia Ludowa seemingly supposed to have the sympathetic to the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, we had-- we were watching at night. It was my night to watch. At night, if we see somebody approaching-- because if somebody comes in, we should know and run into the hiding. I forgot to mention also that in the chicken coop, he built a hole for in case of-- Emergency.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of an inspection of Germans coming to the village to stuff us in into that things. It was really not made for 10 people. It was just barely-- when we had to go in there, it was really that we could stand one against the other in a standing position. That's about all-- also, no air whatsoever. Now, when-- that night that I was watching, I did see some really fire, pieces of bumps, so to say. And I could hear it, swishes coming into the fields. I really did not know, or I did not experience that kind of fightings, or anything of approaching an army, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was scared. What do I do I do? So I walked over. The man was sleeping in that room where I was sitting at the window.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I woke him up, and I called him. I barely touched him. I was afraid that he may hit me by waking in middle of the night. And I said, 'Mister, Mister, I-- I see some fire falling in our fields. And I don't know what it is.' He says, 'Good, good, you woke me up.' I said, 'I'm sorry. [INAUDIBLE].' 'No, no, no, no. It's good, it's good. You did right. You did right.' He walks out-- get dressed, walks out. And he says, 'The Russians are closing in. The front is here.'

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, 'Tell everybody to get dressed, whatever it is, because I want you to get into the barn and into the cellar.' So we went into the barn and cellar. A few hours later, he came, and-- and he called my brother. He liked him very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, [? 'Ignacz, ?] Eli, you always dreamed to have a machine gun. A German is here now in the house. He wants a pair of slacks. He wants to change to civilian clothes. He wants to run away. He wants to give you the machine gun for it.' My brother was ready to give him the pants, a machine gun. Everybody was starting to say, [? 'You ?] look a bit older than him. Stop it. This front can move east and west, and then he'd come back that he left here the machine gun.' We didn't want it. We sent him away. Another couple hours later, the peasant knocked at the door from the cellar. And he says again to my brother, 'I want you to come up. The Russians are in our backyards.' So of course, everybody wanted to run up. He said, 'Oh, no, no, no, no. Only one at the time, one.' So that's the way it started, and we were liberated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Russians accept you right away as-- as Jews who were in hiding and survived? Or were they skeptical?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happens to be that at that place, there was the general kitchen that they had, the field kitchen from the army. And we were feeded properly. Of course, there was good food. Of course, we were very careful not to eat, which was good, because otherwise, we would have gotten sick from the fat thing. But they were not-- they did not have a chance to ask-- we did not have a chance even to see him properly. They moved out. They eat, and they move. That was the front line. They just broke through. We did not see nobody. Horses and just tanks and just-- were just moving.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were staying. We did not know what to do. So when-- this was at the daytime. Now, let's say in the evening around 4, 5 o'clock, in January, the day is short-- the fighting started really. The trenches, they started to open up the fire, one against the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the village that we were-- part of the village that we were in were just in between no man's land. In other words, in the one part was the Germans, in one part was the Russians. And they were fighting, just one between the other. And then we-- and-- and those houses in that middle were just burning hell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we could not stay in the barn, because there was no fireproof cellars. That was just simply it would blow up, like, in no time. So we said to the peasant, 'We want to get out.' And we went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went walking. And that [? normal-- ?] falling, full of ammunition, you know, bullets, going, swishing to our heads. And we walked then into another village. And that other village had already the Russians established, so to say-- it was the second line in command.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of my cousins said, 'We are Jews.' So I said, 'Jews what? No, no, no.' [? Starting to ?] say too much. We didn't want to say anything, and we starting to be in that village. So you stayed. Was that village now free under the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was free under the Russians. But still, they were-- they were skeptical, and they were reluctant to accept us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We spread. We said, it's no good that we walk 10 people together, and we spread out in three groups again, how we would walk. Then we did not say that we're Jews. Just the opposite-- we said we're not Jews. We did not say 'not Jews.' We said we came from another town which was actually bombarded, and there's fighting going on there, and that-- you know, whatever. There were some other not local people. There were out-of-town people in that village. And seemingly, they were from Warsaw. And they said-- they were starting to murmur to each other, 'Those are Jews. Those are Jews.' OK, we were not too comfortable about it. But we felt there's no way now to go to any discussion or anything to run to hiding. The Russians are here. Then after a couple days, a day staying in that village, we went back to our hometown that we were born and raised, into the hometown, Zarki.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zarki. How long did it take you to get from where you were to Zarki?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- probably, we were hitchhiking with the Russians, and they took us into town. Must have been a few hours just to get into the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell us-- what did you find in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We really found in the town really disappointment. Nobody was waiting for us. Nobody was greeting us. The war was not over for us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had no place to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to go to your own home?

speaker: interviewee
text: The house that we had was a nice home. It was-- somebody lived in the house. You cannot just simply--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was living in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the name of the people, but I know that they lived there. And-- But they did not move out in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they would not move out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Eventually, my mother pleaded with them. She said, 'Look, I do not need four rooms. What I really need is just that one room to be able to sleep. And the kitchen, we can use, just to-- to share it.'

speaker: interviewee
text: So he really could not refuse. So he said, 'How could I refuse you,' he said, 'Mrs. Zborowski? You are actually the owner of the house. Can I refuse you?'

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, 'That's nice of you.' So my mother even said not the question of no. And there was plenty Jewish homes that they were empty. Maybe they were not this nice as this one, but there were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we moved in into the house. And Eli, of course, went out to school to [? Lodz, ?] to the second largest city. And I stayed there with my mother, and my uncle, and aunt. And then in May, my brother and sister, they already left Poland, and I remained with my mother in the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your brother and sister go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seemingly, they were told that they-- under false papers, under illegal way, they would be able to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they set out for Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: They set out for Palestine, but they got stuck in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you and your mother, what do you do in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I tried to do is, being a young boy, I tried to register to school. But somehow, somewhere, there was a reluctance to-- to be accepted even to-- to finish my public, or to go to high school. I was not welcome. I was not welcome to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you have anything that you have hidden before you left that you were thinking about, or that your father might have left you-- some ideas about things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. We did. It happened to be that I was the one that my father took me to-- to show where he is hiding. And he-- I mean, relatively speaking, going back today, living today in America, it's-- what means money? I mean, it's so relative to say how much means money money? But in those days, I felt my father was hiding boxes of gold, I mean, and coins, and all kind of precious things. And that was the main reason why my mother wanted to put the foot in the door and to be able to get into the house. And we succeeded, in a way, that after a few months, the Polish family moved out from the house. There was plenty Jewish other homes. And we occupied the four rooms. And one of my aunts-- and the house was really plundered. You could see that the plaster of the walls was removed and searching for-- for valuables. Because this was Zborowski house, and that they were looking for valuables.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was not in a hurry to go to look for these possessions, because I knew it's not there. If they were just taking off the-- the mortar from the walls, the bricks were removed. So they-- I'm sure that they found it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, one of my mother's sisters kept on insisting, 'Go down. Look. You know exactly in what corner it is.' And I went down. It was in a corner. Let's say this is the basement. And the corner was there, and I started to dig. And I digged underneath the foundation. And this was put underneath the foundation. Now, underneath the foundation-- again, on the outside of the foundation, I couldn't dig from the outside, because there were the stairs there-- like the stairs, let's say, in this house. They were underneath. So I was digging, digging. It was water come in. I could not find it. I could not find anything. And I got tired, I came back up. Went again down and digged again a little bit. Again, couldn't. One day, my aunt was really persistent, and she said, 'I want you to continue.' And I continued, I continued. And sure enough, I found it. It was 10 boxes with gold and all kind of precious metal. And the-- it was actually-- the 10 boxes were rusted. And the reason why I couldn't find it, it is because seemingly, I was told, that the soil is moving after years. But I found everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were able to take those things out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took it out, everything. Yes.
